claims.text: Andrew Dawson, a 34-year-old TikTok user living in British Columbia, became the center of a viral 2022 internet mystery through videos starting in April 2022.

evidence.quote:
his videos reflected
the life of a nice normal guy 34 years
old living in British Columbia

claims.rationale: Biographical framing — TikTok user from British Columbia who posted the mountain videos — is corroborated by the host's on-location geolocation work later in the episode (cedarside Regional Park signage, BC-5 / Yellowhead Highway).

claims.text: On April 9th 2022 Andrew filmed a large humanoid figure on a mountain ridge that he said was too big to be a normal person.

evidence.quote:
Andrew was driving with a
friend when he spotted something on the
ridge of whistler's peak in Alberta
Canada

claims.rationale: Host reports the video exists and gives a date. Host later corrects the location to canoe Mountain on BC-5 (not Whistler Peak in Alberta), so the location Andrew supplied was already wrong. Omit directTruth — the 'saw a giant' portion is the premise the whole investigation scrutinizes.

claims.text: The giant Andrew filmed was just the top of a cell phone tower on canoe Mountain, not Whistler Peak.

evidence.quote:
this mountain reported as Whistler Peak
but it's not that Ridge is actually
canoe Mountain

Andrew thought looked like a
giant was just the top of a cell phone
tower

claims.rationale: Host geolocates from cedarside Regional Park signage in Andrew's own video, names the correct mountain and identifies the tower, and explains the tower's apparent 'disappearance' as a change in viewing angle as the highway veers west. Concrete, checkable, unrebutted in-episode.

dependencies.rationale: [debunks] Identifies the filmed 'giant' as mundane infrastructure.

claims.text: Andrew was stopped on a public road in a national park by someone he described as a government agent telling him he was trespassing.

evidence.quote:
he was stopped by some type of
government agent

claims.rationale: The encounter is on camera. Host later casts doubt on the 'government agent' characterization (aftermarket exhaust, casual tone, car parked with motor running in front of house — 'huge fail' as surveillance tradecraft). Whether it happened: likely. Whether it was government: doubted.

claims.text: Reddit debunkers' claim that the CIA does not operate in Canada is wrong.
claims.rationale: Host surfaces the Reddit position specifically to reject it. 'CIA operates everywhere' is rhetorical rather than a substantiated operational claim — CIA HUMINT in allied countries is heavily constrained by liaison arrangements. Omit directTruth because reality sits between the two extremes.

claims.text: The helicopters Andrew filmed carrying objects over the mountain were heli-logging — commercial tree removal from inaccessible areas — not government activity.

evidence.quote:
this is when
helicopters are brought in to remove
trees from inaccessible areas

claims.rationale: Heli-logging is a routine commercial operation in British Columbia forestry and fits the observed footage (helicopters carrying long objects over forested terrain). No specific operator/date cited, so small residual uncertainty.

claims.text: The 'ominous black structure built overnight' was the Jasper Sky Tram, a tourist tramway built in 1964.

evidence.quote:
the Jasper Sky tram even how
did he build that overnight they didn't
it was built in 1964.

claims.rationale: Jasper Sky Tram is a real, documented aerial tramway opened in 1964 (Jasper, Alberta). Host also notes Andrew is local to the area, so the 'built overnight' framing is almost certainly performative rather than sincere.

claims.text: Three weeks after the mountain saga began, Andrew posted an 'official update' claiming all his videos were scripted fakes for entertainment.

evidence.quote:
all of
the videos that I post all the videos
that I posted were scripted they're just
fake

claims.rationale: Retraction video is quoted in the episode. Whether it is a sincere retraction or a performance is the host's downstream question — omit directTruth here; that judgment lives in the retraction-of-retraction and host-verdict claims.

claims.text: Ten days later Andrew retracted the retraction, posting that 'my videos weren't fake' and that he couldn't be forced to be silent.

evidence.quote: my videos weren't they weren't fake

claims.rationale: Retraction-of-retraction is well-attested from contemporaneous TikTok posts quoted in the episode. directTruth reflects that the post happened, not that 'videos were real' is true.

dependencies.rationale: [logical] Directly negates the earlier scripted-fake retraction.

claims.text: Andrew Dawson reportedly died on July 1st 2022 at age 34, with his obituary appearing the same day in the Campbell River Mirror.

evidence.quote:
July 1st about six
weeks after Andrew's final Tick Tock

Andrew Dawson passed
away on July 1st 2022 he was only 34

claims.rationale: Obituary in Campbell River Mirror is reported by the host and two people allegedly confirmed the death. But host flags that the newspaper accepts obituaries without a death certificate and that independent records are thin. Omit directTruth — the episode itself treats the death as contested.

claims.text: Anyone can submit an obituary to the Campbell River Mirror website without a death certificate, undermining the obituary as evidence of death.

evidence.quote:
anyone can post on that
newspaper's website without a death
certificate

claims.rationale: Many community-newspaper obituary portals in North America accept paid submissions without verification — plausible on prior probability even though the host does not cite the paper's submission terms directly.

dependencies.rationale: [undercuts] Weakens the obituary as proof of death.

claims.text: The host's final verdict is that every mountain video is explainable as a hoax, but Andrew's death itself is genuinely uncertain.

evidence.quote:
Andrew's death is the
strangest part

faking
your own death isn't easy

claims.rationale: Explicit host verdict — all the mountain pieces debunkable, the death portion ambiguous. Opinion claim about the host's stance, not a factual proposition about reality.

dependencies.rationale: Tower debunk feeds the overall hoax framing.
Sky-tram debunk feeds the overall hoax framing.
